FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study explicitly; N=9,066 children aged 9-10 at baseline, 21 recruitment sites, ABCD data release 4.0 used.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical bidirectional longitudinal analysis using cumulative logistic regression; psychiatric symptoms predicting screen time one year later and screen time predicting psychiatric symptoms one year later.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Social Psychiatry and Psychiatric Epidemiology (2024).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: social screen time composite = texting + social media + video chatting (exclusively phone/SM/digital-socializing items). Results report OR estimates for social screen time as DV (e.g., internalizing clinical symptoms predicting weekday social screen time OR=0.92; rule-breaking predicting high weekday social screen time OR=1.14) and as IV predicting psychiatric symptoms (weekday social OR=1.11, 95% CI 1.10-1.12 predicting total psychiatric problems). Individual-trait outcomes on the other side: internalizing, externalizing, attention symptoms (CBCL).

FT_IC3.evidence: Numerous OR estimates with 95% CIs reported throughout main text, figures, and supplementary tables for both directions of the association.

FT_IC4.evidence: Same as IC3; all numeric results reported via ordinal logistic regression ORs with confidence intervals.

FT_EC7_reason: Bidirectional design: screen time (including social media/texting composite) appears as both DV (psychiatric symptoms predicting screen time) and IV (screen time predicting future psychiatric symptoms). EC7 requires SM as DV in every analysis with no SM-as-IV analysis anywhere; that condition is not met.
notes: Social screen time composite = texting + social media use + video chatting; qualifies under Step 1 as exclusively phone/SM items. Nonsocial screen time (YouTube + TV shows + video games) does not qualify on its own, but social screen time alone is sufficient for IC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: Explicitly uses the ABCD dataset; multisite longitudinal study, N~7107 at baseline, ages 9-10, data from NDA (abcdstudy.org).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: linear mixed-effects models and one-sample/two-sample Mendelian randomization on ABCD data examining causal relationships between screen use and cognitive/behavioral/brain outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Advanced Science (2024).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: SNS (visiting social networking sites) and Text (texting on a cell phone, tablet, or computer) and VC (video chatting) are separately measured phone/SM-specific variables with their own coefficients. Figure 2 and Table S1 report β coefficients for SNS predicting picture vocabulary (β=-4.28 and -1.31 at two timepoints), rule-breaking behavior (β=0.73 and 0.42), and aggressive behavior. Text predicts picture vocabulary (β=-0.93 and -1.56). Individual-trait outcomes on the other side: cognitive scores (NIH Toolbox) and behavioral problems (CBCL).

FT_IC3.evidence: Beta coefficients with standard errors and p-values reported throughout Figure 2, Table S1 (LME), Table S2 (MR), and Figures 3, 6 for SNS, Text, VC, TVM, Video, and Game screen use types.

FT_IC4.evidence: Same as IC3; quantitative results reported for multiple screen types across cognitive and behavioral outcomes at two timepoints, plus MR causal estimates.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen use variables (including SNS, Text, VC) are IVs predicting cognitive and behavioral outcomes as DVs throughout main analyses. EC7 requires SM as DV in every analysis with no SM-as-IV anywhere; SNS and Text are IVs in primary LME and MR analyses.
notes: Six screen activity types analyzed separately: TVM, Video, Game, Text, SNS, VC. SNS and Text are qualifying phone/SM variables. Reverse MR analyses also present (cognition/behavior predicting screen use) but primary direction is SM/screen as IV. INCLUDE.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study explicitly; N=6,143 adolescents from 21 sites, ABCD Study 5.1 data release, ages 10-15.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using Bayesian hierarchical linear models examining association between fMRI reward processing (MID task) and VGAQ scores across years 2, 3, and 4.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Behavioral Addictions (2024).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable with its own coefficient anywhere in the paper (main tables, Table 3, Table 4, supplementary). The only gaming-side variable is VGAQ (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire), which measures generic video game addiction (single-player or online multiplayer, platform unspecified). Step 2: VGAQ is not a composite including phone/SM items; it is a gaming-only scale. Per binding clarification, VGAQ fails IC2 unless paired with a separately-measured phone/SM variable. No such pairing exists in this paper.

notes: Entire paper is about gaming addiction (VGAQ) and neural reward processing (fMRI MID task). No phone or social media variable measured or reported. VGAQ = platform-unspecified video game addiction scale. Exclusion code: FT-EC2.
